DECLARATION IN SUPPORT OF OPPOSITION TO DEFT'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION FILED BY PLAINTIFF GOVEA, CHRISTOPHER
MINI-MINUTES FOR AUG-20-2007 9:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT UNITED STATES PIPE AND FOUNDRY COMPANY'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. ARGUED AND THE COURT ADOPTS AND MODIFIES ITS TENTATIVE RULING: DENIED. THERE IS AN AGREEMENT BETWEEN THE PARTIES TO ARBITRATE BASED UPON PLAINTIFF REMAINING ON THE JOB AFTER THE RECEIPT OF THE DISPUTE RESOLUTION PROGRAM. CRAIG V. BROWN & ROOT INC. (2000) 84 CAL.APP.4TH 416. THE AGREEMENT IS ONE OF ADHESION GIVEN THE MANNER IN WHICH IT WAS IMPOSED AND PLAINTIFF'S INABILITY TO EFFECT ANY OF THE TERMS. THE AGREEMENT IS CONTRARY TO THE MANDATE OF ARMENDARIZ V. FOUNDATION HEALTH PSYCHCARE SERVICES (2000) 24 CAL.4TH 83. IT IS BOTH PROCEDURALLY AND SUBSTANTIVELY UNCONSCIONABLE. THE AGREEMENT PRECLUDES THE ARBITRATOR FROM "SUBSTITUT[ING] HIS/HER JUDGMENT FOR THE COMPANY RULES, PRACTICES AND PERFORMANCE. THE ARBITRATOR MUST ENFORCE, AND CANNOT DEVIATE IN ANY WAY FROM, THESE RULES AND PROCEDURES." NO JUDICIAL OFFICER WOULD EVER BE SUBJECT TO SUCH A RESTRAINT. THE AGREEMENT ALSO CARVES OUT CERTAIN CLAIMS. AN ANALYSIS OF THOSE CLAIMS DEMONSTRATES A LACK OF MUTUALITY. CERTAIN OF THE CARVED OUT CLAIMS ARE SUBJECT TO MANDATORY STATUTORY REQUIREMENTS THAT EFFECTIVELY PRECLUDE ARBITRATION - UNEMPLOYMENT COMPENSATION, WORKPLACE SAFETY, PENSION AND BENEFIT PLANS, CRIMINAL CHARGES AND LABOR RELATIONS. AT THE SAME TIME, THE AGREEMENT GIVES THE EMPLOYER THE RIGHT TO FILE SUIT AND AVOID ARBITRATION FOR THE CERTAIN CLAIMS AN EMPLOYER WOULD HAVE AGAINST AN EMPLOYEE. IN CONTRAST, THE MOST LIKELY EMPLOYEE CLAIMS REQUIRE ARBITRATION. THE POTENTIAL DISCOVERY LIMITATIONS STANDING ALONE ARE NOT PROCEDURALLY UNCONSCIONABLE. JUDGE: PATRICK J. MAHONEY, REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR #8600 **[ORDER VACATING THE RULING THE MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION AND TAKING UNDER SUBMISSION FILED ON SEPTEMBER 19, 2007]**
MINI-MINUTES FOR SEP-17-2007 9:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, COURT VACATES ITS SEPTEMBER 17, 2007 RULING ON THE MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION AND NOW TAKES THE MATTER UNDER SUBMISSION. COURT (JUDGE MAHONEY), HAVING TAKEN THE MATTER UNDER SUBMISSION, NOW ISSUES "ORDER GRANTING THE MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION AND A STAY OF THE PROCEEDINGS".

Court Vacates Its September 17, 2007 Ruling On The Motion To Compel Arbitration And Now Takes The Matter Under Submission
Off Calendar Sep-21-2007 Text Ruling

Motion To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Sep-17-2007 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT UNITED STATES PIPE AND FOUNDRY COMPANY'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION.
APPEARANCES: WILLIAM G. LEWIS, ESQUIRE (LEWIS JOHNSON, 415.538.1475) APPEARING FOR PLAINTIFF. ADRIENNE E. NELSON, ESQUIRE (SEYFARTH SHAW, 415.397.2823) APPEARING FOR DEFENDANT.
RULING: ARGUED AND THE COURT ADOPTS AND MODIFIES ITS TENTATIVE RULING: DENIED. There is an agreement between the parties to arbitrate based upon plaintiff remaining on the job after the receipt of the dispute resolution program. Craig v. Brown & Root Inc. (2000) 84 Cal.App.4th 416.
The agreement is one of adhesion given the manner in which it was imposed and plaintiff's inability to effect any of the terms.
The agreement is contrary to the mandate of Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83. It is both procedurally and substantively unconscionable. The agreement precludes the arbitrator from "substitut[ing] his/her judgment for the Company rules, practices and performance. The arbitrator must enforce, and cannot deviate in any way from, these rules and procedures." No judicial officer would ever be subject to such a restraint.
The agreement also carves out certain claims. An analysis of those claims demonstrates a lack of mutuality. Certain of the carved out claims are subject to mandatory statutory requirements that effectively preclude arbitration - unemployment compensation, workplace safety, pension and benefit plans, criminal charges and labor relations. At the same time, the agreement gives the employer the right to file suit and avoid arbitration for the CERTAIN claims an employer would have against an employee. In contrast, the most likely employee claims require arbitration.
The potential discovery limitations standing alone are not procedurally unconscionable. (302/PJM) JUDGE: PATRICK J. MAHONEY, REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR 8600
**[ORDER VACATING THE RULING THE MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION AND TAKING UNDER SUBMISSION FILED ON SEPTEMBER 19, 2007]**
